speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Simcha-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I call myself here Sam. Now, we were on the way to Lutsk. Yeah, so we arrived after two, three days, into Lublin, and then we took another [NON-ENGLISH] train. And then by foot, we crossed the River Bug-- we crossed it by foot. And we arrived after a week, we arrived in Lutsk. And there, after four days, the-- the Red Army came, and the Russians came in. I remember vividly, I went out on the highway. And many people came out. And then they start rolling the heavy Russian tanks, with the tankers, with the soldiers. And they were greeted by, especially by the Jews, by others, too, by the Ukrainians-- they were greeted as the redeemers, their friend. And I was in Russia. Now, what happened-- and her brother-in-law was in Russia with her parents. What happened to my wife, as I mentioned, this-- her sister that was married to the-- to that young man in Lutsk, she came over. She stole-- she stole the-- the border was half open. People were lying-- the Germans, when they came over to the border, the Germans robbed them. They beat them up. But they let them cross. But the Russians didn't let them. So the people were lying on the border pass, and between no-man's land-- with the children-- on the-- in rain and the cold. And the Russians used to say, the whiners out-- go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: But people tricked them. When they were caught by the Russians, they asked them, where do you want to go? I want to go to Warsaw. They said the whiners out. So they-- they went-- they let them go to Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard other people that they collected a lot of them. And they started to march and sing and march. And they let them in from time to time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So her sister, stole the-- went through the border. He came to Lutsk. She saw him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not only this, she went back to bring her belongings. She was a very daring girl. I remember, I-- I-- I traveled with her to Bialystock, to the city. I wanted also to go back to see my family, maybe help them come over. But in Bialystock, I lost her. And I was helpless-- I didn't have money, I was young. And I went back to Lutsk. She went back home, and she brought her youngest sister. And the-- two sister-- another sister was bought by somebody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she came--my girlfriend came-- and was also in Lutsk. And what happened with the younger sister-- in the house of the older sister. What happened to them is very interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her youngest sister, the second, she lives here. She wanted to go back-- a lot of people wanted to go back to Poland, to Germany. First of all, it was harder-- there were a lot of refugees. There was no work, and so on. They lived in synagogues and public places-- no housing. And the people stuck to the border. They didn't want to go deep into Russia, because they were--they thought the war will be over soon. They will return. They were afraid to lose. They were proposed Soviet citizenship. People didn't want to take it. They were afraid they wouldn't be able to return to Poland after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happens in May-- in May-June, 1940, all those that registered to go back to Poland-- all those that refused to accept Soviet passport were arrested by the KGB, put on trains, and sent to Siberia. Unwillingly, they save them, because the-- and a year later, the Germans came. So when they came after her younger sister, they came without the youngest sister, they took her to-- or, she joined herself. She didn't want to let her sister go. So she joined her. They went to the wagon. And they were sent deep in the taiga in a village where they couldn't leave. It wasn't a camp, it wasn't a labor camp. But it was half-arrested to live there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The two girls-- she was 17. The other was maybe 14. And they survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest sister-- that married the man from-- [NON-ENGLISH], the man from Lutsk, they didn't take it. She was a citizen there, her husband was born there, and lived there. And what happened when the Germans attacked?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were overrun-- they had already a child. They were overrun. I think they went back towards Lutsk, and they all perished. The two girls were taken by the KGB, by the Russians, to Siberia. The two girls somehow survived. And I met them later in Asia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's continue your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my story is I-- I saw in Lutsk, I saw that it's no place to stay. I don't have work, and so on. I registered freely-- there was a registration for work in Russia-- I registered to go there to Russia for work. And it was in 1940, I remember, I-- January-- 1st of January, a very cold night. I took a sled. I rented a sled. My girlfriend walked with me to the station and we said good-bye. And I went into the train. And this was a train. They called it a [NON-ENGLISH]-- a freight train, with about 2,000 refugees, mainly Jews, Polish Jews, going into Russia for work. It was very interesting. Each-- each wagon, there were about 40 to a wagon. We had on coats-- woolen coats, on planks-- we slept in the middle of the wagon. It was a little-- a little stove, an iron stove, for warmth. We used to get rations-- bread. Some, from time to time, we stopped at a train station for a meal, for a hot meal. And it took us about two weeks. And from time to time, the train stopped. And they took down people to different factories and plants for work. And we were at the station of Rozan-- we had the meal-- ate a meal. A young man appeared. He was the KGB emissary at a factory-- letter factory-- in a little town of Spassk Ryazan Oblast. And he choose eight young men-- I have a picture there of the eight young men. It was in the middle of the night. They took us in a truck, covered us with furs, and we came into this factory. They greeted us in the mess hall, we had drink. And they brought us to hotel-- it was the only hotel-- on clean sheets. After 18 days and this in the train, it was a relief. And then they took us around a few days over the city to show us. And they-- we went to a shower in the factory. And they gave us new clothes. It's amazing-- people don't believe it, but this is the truth. I have a poem about it. They gave us new underwear, new suits, new jackets, cotton jackets. And they took us to the shop-- to the shoemaker shop. And they took measures. And after a few days, we had all new, hand-sewn leather boots. We have the photograph. And then they eventually settled us to work. One was a porter there. So he was-- he was packing the letter. One was in the metal shop. One-- I had a friend, I made a friend on the way, he was a student. He was assigned to the lavatory. And I was assigned to-- and to the shoemakers. First of all, I knew to stitch on the machine. So I stitched these sandals. And then they-- And so it's a woman's work. They gave me a-- it was a small shoemaker's shop-- about 10 shoemakers sewing for the leaders, for the workers, and so on. And they gave me under the apprenticeship of a shoemaker. And I got 100 rubles a month of apprenticeship wages. And he gave me, also-- because I helped him. And after a half a year, I knew how-- I knew how to make a pair of boots. But then the war broke out. Another thing-- another thing-- as soon as we came, I and my friend, a new friend-- his name was Joseph Weingarten, I have a picture of him-- we went to study. We wanted to join at night high school. I did my elementary school in Poland, but I didn't go to high school. And there was a high school-- an evening high school for adults. [NON-ENGLISH], it was on the edge of the city. We came over. We didn't know well Russian. But they said, sit down, you will learn. And we stay--we were accepted as free listeners. But then by the end, we made--we made grade nine. And then grade 10. I was strong in literature and language, he was strong in mathematics and physics, and so on. We helped each other. And in June 1941, we graduated. We have the matriculation here. And then we decided to go study-- we applied for a teacher's institute. When the war-- when the Germans attacked Russia, there was a law, you cannot-- you were bound to your place of work. Unless the war industry needs you, or unless you go to study to an institute of higher learning-- university.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were lucky. We applied to our institute-- the pedagogic institute. And we left Spassk. We went to Kazin-- I and my friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meanwhile, when I was at Spassk, I got the address of my girlfriend-- of my wife. She was in Siberia in that village with her sister. They were in bad shape-- they were-- it was hard. I sent them a few packages-- some boots and so on. And when we came to Kazin, we enrolled in that institute. We did a foolish thing, which I regret-- we left the institute. We said-- they freed the people. They freed the people. As soon as the war broke out, Poland made an agreement with Russia. And they freed all those Polish citizens that were intent--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's just clarify, what had happened-- just explain-- the Russians had part of Poland. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Russians had part of Poland. The Germans, in 1941, treacherously attacked. And they marched forward. They took over all those areas. They went as far as near Moscow, and the from the south up to Stalingrad. So-- the-- so the Russians needed help. And they made an agreement with Britain, and they needed American help. And they made an-- they made an agreement with the Polish government in exile, in London. To further-- and one of the points of the agreement was to free all Polish citizens, arrested or interned, or sent in those villages. So all of them were freed. And these two girls were also freed. And all of them, most of them, went down-- streamed down to the south of Russia, to the Asian part, because the climate is there. I don't know, be close to Polish army-- was concentrating there, in Russia. They formed a Polish army.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they streamed down. And we were swept with this thing. We decided to leave the place where we settled, or we started to study. And we left by train to Siberia, from Siberia down, to the city of Almaty. This is the capital city of Kazakhstan. And then we went to Osh.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard. We couldn't settle ourselves. I found out then-- I found out the address of my girlfriend and her sister. After they were freed from the taiga, from the-- from that village. They went by a river, they went to the city of Tomsk. And there, they were homeless. They were sleeping in a shul, in a synagogue. They didn't-- didn't have work. And what happened to her-- they were standing in line for bread . And a Russian woman started to talk to them. And she pitied the two Jewish girls. And she took them in, in her house-- her husband was in the front, had small children, one daughter, worked already in the factory. And she took them in, in the house. And she shared with them her food and everything. She was called--they called her [NON-ENGLISH], Auntie Claudia. And I visited her. I went to Tomsk from-- from Almaty. I visited her-- a very fine woman. And we decided to go-- they decided to go with me to the city of Osh. And this was at-- and we left that woman. And I, my friend, my girlfriend, and her sister, all of us came to Osh. And this was a great disaster. We came to Osh. It's a city in-- in Uzbekistan. It was full of Polish refugees. We hadn't where to stay. I had an uncle-- he wanted to keep me, but he didn't want to keep four people. So we moved out. And we were homeless. The two girls got sick. Also, their friend got sick on the typhus. They were a month in the hospital. They were left out and very helpless. And I was-- one year, one summer, I was working-- I got some work in a park. All this summer, I went barefoot around. I slept under the-- under the sky, in the park, in the grass. And it was hard. But what happened-- help arrived. First of all, the Polish delegation gave out clothes, blankets, shoes. So this was a help. Then they found a friend, as a friend which lived not far in their house-- he was one of the leaders of the bund. They found him in a village in Krygyzstan. And he helped the two girls. I, too, came over. And we met, and we lived together. There, we married. I was working as a shoemaker, going around the villages and repairing in the houses of the Uzbek farmers, of the Ukrainian farmers, repairing their shoes. And they gave me produce. And then I got married. When I went to register, they wanted to take me to the army. I had to register for my wife-- to know that she's married. I bought a pair of boots. We went into the office. But we got married. We had a daughter. Our daughter lives-- and in 1946, after the war-- she was born in 1945-- we went back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me find out something about the marriage. Did you have a civil ceremony?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a civil ceremony only. We went over to the bureau. We-- we have the document, which is in Russian and in Kyrgyz, the marriage document. And then a year later, I got sick. It was-- I had typhus, too. And I came out of hospital, my wife was pregnant. And we had the daughter, Miriam. She was born there. Our friend was with us. He helped us. He knew how to arrange things-- like he arranged that I work for a factory shoemaker. In reality, I worked at home. And the farmers brought in their shoes. I repaired them, and they brought us food, and so on, milk, and other produce. And that's the way we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: During your time in Russian Asia, did you hear any news of what was happening in Poland either generally? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We began to hear the news. We couldn't believe it, that people were exterminated and so on. But then we came to Poland-- we returned in 1946. A whole train again-- took us a month-- traveled back to Poland. And we settled in Poland in Lower Silesia. This was the part that they took-- they occupied-- Poland occupied from Germany-- took away from Germany. Some-- and we lived there. But then I went to Otwack. And let me tell you, what I saw, and what I heard. I came to Otwack a few times. The whole area, however, they had three-- they had three sections. The ghetto in 1940-- they introduced the ghetto. They had the Judenrat, they appointed a Judenrat, and they appointed a head of the Jewish police-- a young man. The ghetto was mainly that Jewish section-- the poor Jewish section-- around the bazaar where I grew up. This was the main ghetto. Then there was-- over the-- the city was divided by the rail tracks. Over the other side of the rail tracks, in the [NON-ENGLISH] section, there was another small ghetto, an enclave, where the police, the Judenrat was-- the Jewish police station was. And then farther north was another enclave, where there was a Jewish sanatorium for the TB. And there was a sanatorium for mentally sick-- [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was that orphanage, that was bombed. These three institutions.

speaker: interviewer
text: so there were three enclaves. And they were joined by small passages.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, they started to take them away as soon-- very early. They took 200 people to Treblinka to build-- to build the-- the camp. And they started to build the camp. None of them returned. They formed a labor camp near Otwock, in Karczew-- there's a small city near Otwock. They had a labor camp, of which a few people survived. A few people survived from there. And my family-- I got a few letters from them. They said, they are hungry. I should help them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got the letters when you were in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Russia, in Spassk, I got a few postcards from them. And they heard that I was helping my girlfriend in Siberia. So they said, why are you not helping us? We are hungry. And I went there. I arranged a few packages. It was already by the end of the war. I don't know. It was by 1941-- 1942. I don't know if they received it. I know my family survived until the liquidation. Because my auntie, my mother's sister, was in our house. She used to live in Warsaw-- she had  a husband, two children, a boy and a girl. The husband, as soon as the war broke out, left and disappeared. She didn't hear from him. We don't know what happened to him. She was left with their  two children. She went back to a village, near Lublin, where she grew up. My grandmother lived there, and my uncle lived there. There were village Jews-- Jews that lived in villages. They were merchants. They were-- they had food-- like with food. And they were also shoemakers, tailors, and so. And they lived among the Poles quite peacefully. She went there. But when they came to arrest all the Jews in that village, she ran out through the window with her two children. And a Pole ran after her, he caught her. And her little son-- he was about 9 or 10 years-- ran over to him, he bit him in the hand. So he left her for a moment. She ran away with her two children. And she came back to Otwock to stay in the ghetto with our family. What happened, they knew that they going to take them away. They didn't know. On August the 18th, the police-- the German regional head of the police came, Brandt. And they knew that they-- photographed them. I found a few photographs. They said they sending them for work, for labor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they photographed them. And I found, in the Jewish community, I found a whole box of photographs. So I took out her mother's photograph-- somebody else's, their photograph. So they knew they were taking them away. So the night before, on the 18th of August, 1942, my auntie took her two children. She was blonde-- she knew Polish, and she was grown up in the village. She took them out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: She took the trains without tickets. And somehow, she came back to that village. And she-- the Poles helped her. She arranged a boy in one village with a farmer-- the girl in another village with a farmer. And she nearby-- and so they were in touch. And they survived. She told me a few things that were important. Once, she traded something-- she used to go to the Warsaw ghetto, and buy things for cheap-- clothes for food, bring food, and bring and then sell the clothes. So she came to the police station to trade-- and she noticed in the-- the train station, not the police-- in the train station, she noticed Germans there-- German soldiers, officers. She didn't know where to hide. There was a big dog in a dog house. And it looks that the dog knew her. So she pushed her in the dog house. The dog was quiet. And she waited until they left, and she went out. The son told me-- he lives in Paris. This farmer that he worked-- he-- attended the cows, and the swines-- he took him Sunday to the church. The Polish boys somehow had a feeling that he was not Polish. And the priest from the pulpit reproached them and said they shouldn't bother him-- he's-- you know, he's Polish, and so on. And so there were Poles that helped them. Otherwise, they couldn't survive-- they survived. Oh, after she went to Israel. She died, I met her in Haifa. And she died there. They said her daughter has two children in New York, the son has four children in Paris. She wrote me, she told me that my sister, Tama, the dark one, came over. She run away. She run away when they liquidated the ghetto. My father with the two girls with the son went to the trains. My mother hid in a basement. And the sister, this time, ran away. And she came to a-- to Sabine, to our city, to that town where I was born. There was still a ghetto. And there she met our neighbor-- our next neighbor. And she heard from this next neighbor that my mother the next day went out-- she couldn't find anybody. She went out, and they arrested her. And they shot her. What happened-- I have a book-- last year in 1939, a book appeared in Warsaw, which is called, in Polish, This is by Calel Perechodnik. He was a policeman-- he was a young man, an intelligent man. He studied for agronome in Paris in France. He came back before the war. He was in the ghetto. He married, had a child. And he joined the police, the Jewish police. And he described everything, all the scenes of what happened on that day of August in 1942, where they gathered all the-- 8,000 Jews before the-- in a place before the trains. And they waited all day-- the carpenters made steps to go into the freight wagons. They pushed-- they pushed them in. And there were terrible scenes. People asked Poles to the gate to bring them poison. To go over to the drug store-- to the Polish drug man-- and bring them poison to poison themselves. Some people described-- a very aristocratic woman came over, and she said she wants to join. She looked like a Pole-- she wants to join. They didn't believe her, the Germans-- the Ukrainians were there. But they said, she wanted  to join with her people. They pushed her in. They-- the Jewish policeman, there were 100 Jewish policemen with their commandant, they helped to chase the Jews to bring them to the train. And they guarded them with Ukrainians. They hoped that they would survive. They were standing, and their wife and children were put aside. So they hoped that their wives and children will survive. In the last minute, the German commandant said to push them in-- push their wives and children. And the 100 policemen were standing and looking. They didn't move. They thought they were saving their life. Only 1 of 100, only one policeman survived. His family goes into the wagon. He threw his police hat, his police-- and he went into the wagon. And this is why he regrets. He calls the book Now what happened-- what happened to him was the policeman were taken to a camp. Finally, they were all shot, even the commandant was shot. He ran away-- he hid himself in Warsaw in hiding with a Polish woman. He was-- and he died in the uprising in 1944, when the Russians were at the shores of the Vistula. They waited, the Poles made an uprising, the Russians didn't intervene. They were all destroyed, and so on. He died there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just wanted to ask one question--

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me finish about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hiding, in three months, he wrote this book-- the memoir of what happened in the Otwock ghetto. He sent it to our friend, the Polish friend, in Otwock. He kept it. After the war, his brother survived in Russia, came back. He took to the original copy to the Yad Vashem, to Israel. He made a copy and left a copy in the Jewish Historic Institute in Warsaw. And it was lying there. It was quoted here and there-- in quotation-- part chapters in the [NON-ENGLISH], in the memorial book. But in 1939, they published it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's just review what happened to your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one more, let me add, the sister Tama came there to the village, to the auntie. To the-- to the auntie. And she told her about my mother-- that my mother left her-- a neighbor told my mother left. And she was shot there on the spot, together with others. Tama-- she arranged Tama with another farmer. And what he did-- the farmer did-- she sent her to trade. There was a working labor camp of Czech Jews. And they were very well off, with nice clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the farmers traded for food over the-- over the fence for food-- for clothes, for watches, and so on. She sent Tama to trade it. And some guard recognized her, he chased her, and he shot her before the doors.

speaker: interviewee
text: What he tells there is that after they sent away after the 9th and sent away the 8,000, about 4,000 were collected and gathered from basements, from attics, and so on. And they were put in the Jewish police station under the outside, in the open. And the people we're singing, and crying, and praying. Twice a week, the gendarmeria came. And they dug up mass graves, and they shot them. They would--he witnessed all this. And then the Jewish policemen were busy to cleaning the ghetto, and son. The Poles, right away when the ghetto was out, the Poles robbed-- they attacked the place. And they took whatever-- they dragged out whatever they could-- clothes and so on.
